title: UCB — gMG — Thesis
html:
gMG Thesis Tracker — Vinoth's POV

TA: Generalized Myasthenia Gravis (gMG) Client: UCB (Rystiggo + Zilbrysq) Initiated: 2026-04-25 Last reviewed: 2026-04-25 Review cadence: Quarterly (next: July 2026)

How to use this document

Each thesis is a forward-looking bet — not a fact summary. The value is in being wrong and knowing why you were wrong. Update quarterly:

  • Did new evidence strengthen or weaken the thesis?
  • Should the thesis be revised, retired, or replaced?
  • What would you tell a UCB stakeholder if they asked your view on this?

Thesis 1: Vyvgart's first-mover moat is structural, not just behavioral — UCB's best play is the "second-line biologic" positioning

The bet: Vyvgart's dominance in gMG (~40%+ biologic share, $4.15B revenue) is not simply physician inertia that UCB can overcome with better marketing. It is a structural moat built on: (a) 2+ years of real-world prescribing experience, (b) patient community familiarity, (c) CIDP multi-indication halo, and (d) the upcoming seronegative label expansion. UCB will not dislodge Vyvgart from 1L biologic position. Instead, Rystiggo and Zilbrysq should position as the go-to options when Vyvgart fails, when patients want a different mechanism, or when the FcRn class is contraindicated.

Why this matters for Vinoth: This thesis reframes what ZoomRx should be probing in the HCP-Pt study. Rather than measuring whether UCB is "winning" against Vyvgart, the study should illuminate: (a) What triggers a switch away from Vyvgart? (b) When switching, do HCPs default to same-class (another FcRn inhibitor like Rystiggo) or different-class (complement via Zilbrysq)? (c) Is there a meaningful "Vyvgart-refractory" patient segment that UCB can own?

Evidence FOR:

  • ZoomRx HCP-Pt data: 31 Vyvgart mentions, 17 prescriptions vs. Rystiggo's 13 mentions, 3 prescriptions across 40 conversations
  • HCPs consistently position Vyvgart first: "of the branded lot, I'm going to VYVGART"
  • argenx revenue trajectory ($2.19B to $4.15B, +90% YoY) shows accelerating adoption, not plateauing
  • Pre-filled syringe further reduced Vyvgart's last remaining convenience gap
  • Seronegative gMG sBLA (PDUFA May 2026) would expand Vyvgart's addressable population by ~15%
  • CIDP approval creates multi-indication prescribing relationship — neurologists who use Vyvgart for CIDP patients are predisposed to use it for gMG

Evidence AGAINST:

  • Imaavy's rapid appearance in ZoomRx data (12 mentions, 3 prescriptions) shows HCPs ARE open to new FcRn options
  • Rystiggo's MuSK+ coverage gives it a niche Vyvgart cannot address (until/unless argenx gets seronegative approval)
  • The gMG market is expanding — market growth could accommodate multiple FcRn winners without requiring head-to-head displacement
  • UCB's dual-brand strategy gives physicians mechanism choice, which could attract practice-level partnerships

Confidence: High. This thesis holds unless UCB invests heavily in a differentiated long-term efficacy dataset or argenx stumbles commercially.

What to watch: Phase 2 final report (May 2026) prescribing rationales. Vyvgart seronegative PDUFA (May 2026). Rystiggo's self-administration adoption in EU/Japan.

Thesis 2: Zilbrysq's daily dosing is a terminal competitive liability — cemdisiran and gefurulimab will compress its window

The bet: Zilbrysq's primary differentiation (the only self-administered C5 inhibitor) is being eroded from two directions: (a) cemdisiran (Regeneron) offers C5 gene silencing with quarterly SC dosing — 90x less frequent than Zilbrysq's daily injection, and (b) gefurulimab (AZ/Alexion) offers monthly SC dosing. Within 18-24 months of cemdisiran's approval, Zilbrysq's new patient starts will decline materially. UCB has a narrow commercial window (2026-2027) to build a loyal patient base before the convenience gap becomes untenable.

Why this matters for Vinoth: If this thesis is correct, UCB should be accelerating Zilbrysq's patient acquisition NOW — not waiting for pipeline threats to materialize. The HCP-Pt data showing zero Zilbrysq prescriptions is alarming in this context. ZoomRx should frame this finding not just as "low awareness" but as "closing window of opportunity." UCB needs to hear this urgency.

Evidence FOR:

  • ZoomRx data: zero Zilbrysq prescriptions across 40 conversations; only 4 mentions, always as part of a laundry list, never as the recommended option
  • Cemdisiran Phase 3 NIMBLE positive (Aug 2025): "rapid, deep and sustained disease control" with SC every 12 weeks. Regulatory filing Q1 2026 — could be approved by late 2026 or early 2027
  • Gefurulimab Phase 3 positive: monthly SC dosing, next-gen within Alexion's own complement portfolio
  • Patient convenience hierarchy is clear: quarterly > monthly > weekly >> daily. Daily SC injection demands the highest patient compliance burden
  • Analyst estimates for Zilbrysq 2026 ($376M) are modest relative to the market opportunity, suggesting the Street already discounts Zilbrysq's growth potential
  • Ultomiris (every-8-week IV) is still growing at 34% YoY — physicians have not yet shifted to SC C5 inhibition at scale, meaning Zilbrysq hasn't proven it can take IV C5 share

Evidence AGAINST:

  • Some patients may prefer daily self-injection control over periodic clinic visits (autonomy argument)
  • Zilbrysq's peptide chemistry is differentiated from monoclonal antibodies — potentially different safety profile, drug interactions, and clinical situations
  • C5 inhibition is maintained even with IVIg/PLEX — important for patients who need rescue therapy
  • If UCB develops a less frequent Zilbrysq formulation (long-acting depot), the convenience gap could narrow
  • Pipeline products face their own approval timelines, safety, and commercial execution risks

Confidence: High. The directional trajectory is clear, though the precise timeline depends on cemdisiran/gefurulimab approval timing.

What to watch: Cemdisiran FDA filing and review timeline. Zilbrysq new patient starts in IQVIA/claims data. UCB's R&D investment in longer-acting zilucoplan formulation.

Thesis 3: The real UCB opportunity is in mechanism-choice partnerships with neurology practices, not brand-by-brand competition

The bet: UCB's unique position (two gMG biologics, two different mechanisms) should be commercialized as a "neurology practice partnership" rather than two separate brand sales efforts. The pitch: "Partner with UCB and you can offer your gMG patients FcRn inhibition OR complement inhibition from a single company — one patient support infrastructure, one reimbursement team, one medical affairs relationship." This positions UCB not as a Vyvgart challenger but as a gMG ecosystem partner for practices that want mechanism flexibility.

Why this matters for Vinoth: This thesis creates a unique ZoomRx research angle. If mechanism-choice partnerships resonate with practices, ZoomRx should study: (a) Do HCPs conceptualize gMG treatment as "choosing a mechanism class" or "choosing a brand"? (b) Is there a segment of practices that would prefer a single pharma relationship covering both mechanisms? (c) What practice-level factors predict openness to UCB's dual-brand model? This could generate a bespoke UCB engagement beyond the current HCP-Pt study.

Evidence FOR:

  • No other gMG company can offer both FcRn and C5 options — this is genuinely unique
  • ZoomRx data shows HCPs listing multiple mechanisms when discussing options: "We have Rystiggo [FcRn], we have Zilbrysq [C5], we have Soliris, Ultomiris [C5], we have Imaavy [FcRn]" — they think in mechanism classes
  • Practice-level consolidation of pharma relationships is a known trend in rare disease
  • UCB's ONWARD patient support program could be a unified access point for both brands
  • Dual-brand promotional efficiency: one MSL/rep relationship covers two products

Evidence AGAINST:

  • HCPs may prefer to pick the best product in each mechanism class regardless of manufacturer (Vyvgart for FcRn, Ultomiris for C5)
  • UCB's field force may not be structured for this bundled approach — may require organizational redesign
  • Payer negotiations are typically brand-specific, not portfolio-bundled (except in rare disease)
  • If one of UCB's brands significantly underperforms, the dual-brand story weakens

Confidence: Medium. This is a strategic thesis, not a data thesis — harder to validate until UCB's commercial team tests the approach.

What to watch: UCB commercial messaging evolution (are they pitching dual-brand or individual brands?). ZoomRx final report — does the conversation data support mechanism-class framing? Practice-level prescribing patterns from claims data.

Thesis 4: Uplizna's B-cell depletion model is the dark horse that could reshape the entire gMG biologic paradigm

The bet: Uplizna (anti-CD19, every 6 months IV) and the broader B-cell depletion approach (rituximab off-label, CAR-T in development) represent a fundamentally different treatment philosophy than FcRn inhibitors and C5 inhibitors. Instead of continuously managing IgG levels or blocking complement, B-cell depletion aims to eliminate the source of pathogenic antibody production. If Uplizna demonstrates robust steroid-free remission rates with just two infusions per year, it will reframe gMG treatment from "chronic management" to "reset and maintain." This would threaten ALL chronic dosing models — Vyvgart, Rystiggo, Zilbrysq, and Imaavy alike.

Why this matters for Vinoth: This is a market structure thesis, not a UCB-specific thesis. If B-cell depletion wins, the gMG biologic market bifurcates into "reset therapies" (Uplizna, rituximab, CAR-T) and "maintenance therapies" (FcRn, C5). The maintenance segment shrinks. ZoomRx should be tracking physician attitudes toward B-cell depletion vs. chronic management to spot this inflection early.

Evidence FOR:

  • Uplizna MINT trial: largest Phase 3 in gMG, first to incorporate steroid taper — FDA approved December 2025
  • ZoomRx Phase 2 data: Uplizna had 5 mentions and 3 prescriptions in just 18 conversations — faster organic uptake than Rystiggo or Zilbrysq had at comparable timepoints
  • "Two doses per year" is a dramatically simpler narrative than any FcRn or C5 dosing schedule
  • Rituximab (anti-CD20) is already widely used off-label for MuSK+ MG, demonstrating B-cell depletion's clinical viability
  • Anti-CD19 depletes more comprehensively than anti-CD20 (targets plasmablasts and some plasma cells, not just B cells)
  • CAR-T (Descartes-08) in development — if a one-time treatment induces durable remission, it's paradigm-changing

Evidence AGAINST:

  • B-cell depletion carries deeper immunosuppression risk than IgG reduction (FcRn) or complement blockade (C5)
  • Longer time to onset — B-cell depletion takes weeks to months for full effect, vs. FcRn inhibitors' rapid IgG reduction
  • Patients on Uplizna cannot be "rescued" with IVIg as easily (since the mechanism is immune reset, not reversible IgG modulation)
  • Rituximab off-label has been available for years and hasn't displaced FcRn/C5 classes — suggesting B-cell depletion is effective for some patients but not universally preferred
  • CAR-T is expensive, high-risk, and likely reserved for refractory patients initially

Confidence: Medium. Uplizna's early signal is real but the paradigm shift depends on long-term steroid-free remission data that will take 2-3 years to mature.

What to watch: Uplizna real-world steroid-free remission rates. MINT long-term extension data. CAR-T clinical trial readouts. Whether neurologists increasingly bifurcate their treatment thinking into "reset" vs. "maintain" approaches.

Thesis 5: The ZoomRx HCP-Pt study's most valuable finding is NOT about Rystiggo/Zilbrysq — it's the Vyvgart switching triggers

The bet: UCB commissioned the HCP-Pt study to understand how their brands are positioned in real conversations. But the most commercially actionable finding will be the specific clinical scenarios, patient complaints, and physician observations that cause them to consider switching FROM Vyvgart. These "Vyvgart friction points" — cycle-off symptom recurrence, access/cost issues, specific AE patterns — are the exact intelligence UCB needs to position Rystiggo and Zilbrysq. ZoomRx should frame the final report around switch triggers, not UCB brand awareness.

Why this matters for Vinoth: This thesis changes the final report framing and creates an upsell opportunity. If ZoomRx identifies 3-4 specific Vyvgart-to-UCB switching triggers, that becomes the basis for: (a) a targeted messaging study (testing which switch trigger messages resonate), (b) a patient segmentation study (which gMG patients are most likely to switch), or (c) a medical affairs engagement (translating switching triggers into clinical scenarios for MSLs to discuss). Each of these is a new project.

Evidence FOR:

  • The ZoomRx data already shows 3 conversations where patients on Vyvgart had ongoing symptoms — these are natural switching opportunity moments
  • One patient on Vyvgart experienced chest pain requiring hospital visits — HCP discussed switching to Imaavy
  • The cycle-off problem is real: "you go four weeks and you start to feel worse" — Imaavy KOL positioned continuous dosing as the solution. Rystiggo could use the same argument.
  • Cost/access discussions appear in multiple conversations — Rystiggo's ONWARD program is actively being used to solve this

Evidence AGAINST:

  • 17 of 40 conversations resulted in Vyvgart prescriptions — the retention rate is very high
  • HCPs who mention Rystiggo/Zilbrysq still often default to Vyvgart for the actual prescription
  • Switching triggers may be too patient-specific to generalize into a scalable finding

Confidence: High. The data exists in the 40 conversations — it's a framing/analysis choice, not a data collection gap.

What to watch: Final report delivery (May 2026). Whether ZoomRx team frames the analysis around switching patterns or brand awareness. Vinoth should guide the team toward switching-trigger framing before the report is written.

Evidence FOR:

Evidence FOR:

Uplizna MINT trial: largest Phase 3 in gMG, first to incorporate steroid taper — FDA approved December 2025
